title: Portable, Mobile, and Stationary Equipment
narrationScript: Walk through any serious MT operation and you will find the same physics wearing three different bodies. A yoke that fits in a tool bag. A power pack on wheels with cables as thick as your wrist. And a bench unit the size of a lathe with a hood over it. This lesson is about why those three classes exist, what each one can and cannot do, and how the big automated systems sequence, control, and police themselves. Start with the simplest question. Why does portable equipment exist at all? Because most of the world's steel will never fit through your shop door. Bridges, pipelines, pressure vessels, crane rails, ship hulls, weld joints forty feet up a column. When the part cannot come to the test, the test goes to the part. Portable means one person can carry it. The electromagnetic yoke is the workhorse, a few pounds of switchable magnet that throws a longitudinal field between its poles. You prove it is working with a lifting check, about ten pounds for alternating current and about forty pounds for direct current, at the pole spacing you will actually use. Permanent magnet yokes need no power at all, which earns them a place where flammable atmospheres rule out electrical gear, though you cannot switch them off and their strength is fixed. Prod kits push current straight through the part for a circular field, fed by small portable packs that typically top out around fifteen hundred amperes. Procedures commonly call for something like ninety to one hundred twenty five amperes per inch of prod spacing, with spacing around six to eight inches. And understand this. A portable unit is not different science. Inside that little pack sits the same step down transformer, the same rectifier, the same tap switch and ammeter you will find in a bench unit. Same circuits, smaller scale, lower duty cycle, and a localized field that forces you to work in overlapping sections. Now mobile equipment. There is a gap between what a hand carried pack can deliver and what a big weldment or heavy shaft demands. Mobile units fill it. Picture the power section of a bench unit on wheels, typically two thousand to six thousand amperes of alternating current or half wave rectified current, towed to the job or rolled between bays. There is no bench, no built in clamps, no bath system. Instead you work with flexible cables. Wrap the cable into a coil for a longitudinal field. Clamp it to the part for a circular shot. Run it through a bore as a central conductor. A remote switch lets you fire from the part, and the same amperage rules you learned for bench techniques apply to the cables. Stationary equipment is the third class. The classic wet horizontal bench holds the part between a headstock and a tailstock for direct contact shots, slides a coil along rails for longitudinal shots, and recirculates a wet particle bath through a hand nozzle, all beside an ultraviolet hood. Full wave rectified current gives it the deepest reach, and three thousand to six thousand amperes is a typical output range. When production volume gets high enough, the bench grows into an automated system, and automation has entry requirements. The parts must be consistent. The technique must be locked down and proven with artificial flaw shims before anyone trusts the machine. Handling, bath application, magnetizing, viewing, demagnetizing, all of it must happen the same way every cycle. And every variable a human inspector would have watched must now be monitored by the machine itself. Sequence matters most. In the continuous method the bath is applied first, then the current fires as the bath drains, while the particles are still mobile on the surface, typically two short shots of about half a second each. Clamp, bathe, shoot, transfer to viewing, demagnetize, unload. Indexing machines run those stations simultaneously on a string of parts. The controls make it repeatable. Tap switches or electronic phase control set the current. Timers set shot length. Quick break circuitry snaps coil shots off sharply to protect sensitivity near the part ends. Interlocks refuse to fire until the part is clamped tight. And the alarms make the whole thing honest. Every shot is compared against preset limits. Low current from a burned contact trips an alarm, stops the line, or diverts the part. Bath flow and ultraviolet intensity are watched the same way. Rejected or suspect parts get marked or gated into a retest lane so they can never mix with good product. And here is the rule that holds it all together. When an alarm fires, every part processed since the last good verification is suspect, and every one of them gets tested again.
imagePrompt: A technician steadies a heavy copper cable from a wheeled magnetizing power pack beside a massive welded steel fabrication, while across the shop a wet horizontal bench unit with a dark inspection hood holds a clamped shaft.

sections.heading: Three Classes, One Physics
sections.body:
Every MT machine you will ever touch does the same two jobs: push enough current or flux through the part to create a usable field, and let you apply particles and see what they do. What changes between a pocket-sized yoke and a forty-foot automated line is not the physics — it is **access, amperage, and volume**.

Three questions sort the hardware:

- **Where is the part?** If it cannot come to you, the equipment must travel.
- **How much current does the technique demand?** Prod, cable-wrap, and central-conductor amperages scale with part size and section thickness.
- **How many parts per hour?** Volume justifies fixtures, recirculating baths, and eventually full automation.

This lesson walks the three classes — portable, mobile, stationary — and then goes inside the automated stationary systems: how they sequence operations, what their controls govern, and how alarm and rejection mechanisms keep a machine honest when no inspector is watching every shot.

sections.heading: Portable Equipment: the Test Goes to the Part
sections.body:
Portable equipment exists because most of the world's steel will never fit through a shop door. Bridge welds, pipelines, in-service pressure vessels, crane booms, ship structure — when the part cannot move, the test travels. Portable means **one technician can carry it**.

The workhorses:

- **Electromagnetic yokes** (AC or AC/DC) throw a longitudinal field between their poles. Performance is proven with a **lifting check — about 10 lb for AC, about 40 lb for DC** — at the pole spacing being used.
- **Permanent-magnet yokes** need no power at all, which suits flammable atmospheres and no-power locations; the trade is a fixed field you cannot switch off, which makes them harder to apply, remove, and control.
- **Prod kits with small power packs** (typically up to roughly 1,500 A) drive current through the part for a circular field — typical procedure values run on the order of **90–125 A per inch of prod spacing**, with spacing commonly 6–8 in. Dry powder with HWDC is the classic pairing for field work on rough surfaces.

Here is the similarity to remember: open a portable pack and you find the same building blocks as a bench unit — step-down transformer, rectifier for DC outputs, tap or stepless current control, an ammeter. Same circuits, same physics, same technique rules. The differences are scale, duty cycle, and a localized field that forces you to inspect **section by section, with overlap**.

sections.caption: The three MT equipment classes — portable yokes and prod kits, mobile cable-fed power packs, and stationary wet horizontal benches — share the same internal circuitry at different scales.

sections.heading: Mobile Equipment: Bench Power on Wheels
Stationary Benches and the Case for Automation
sections.body:
The classic stationary machine is the **wet horizontal bench**: a headstock and tailstock clamp the part for direct-contact circular shots or hold a central conductor; a coil — often riding on rails — provides longitudinal shots; a recirculating pump feeds wet bath through a hand hose; and a hood with UV-A lamps puts fluorescent viewing right at the machine. Outputs of **3,000–6,000 A** are typical, with AC, HWDC, and FWDC available — FWDC reaching deepest. Quick-break circuitry, footswitch firing, clamp-pressure contact plates, and built-in demagnetization round out the package.

When one part number arrives by the thousands, the bench grows into an **automated or semi-automated system** — and automation has entry requirements:

- **Consistent parts.** Mechanized handling and fixed shot geometry assume repeatable size, shape, and material.
- **A locked, proven technique.** Field directions and amperages must be established and demonstrated — typically with QQI shims — before the machine is trusted to repeat them.
- **Mechanized process steps.** Loading, clamping, bath application, magnetization, viewing or automated detection, demagnetization, and unloading each need a station or mechanism.
- **Monitoring in place of human judgment.** Every variable a hands-on inspector would sense — contact quality, delivered current, bath flow, lamp intensity — must be instrumented.
- **Periodic system verification.** Known-defect or QQI-equipped test parts, run at set intervals, prove the whole system still finds what it must.

sections.heading: Sequential Operations
sections.body:
Automated MT lives or dies on **order and timing**. The continuous method requires particles on the part, still mobile, while the field is at strength — so the canonical automated sequence is:

1. **Load and clamp** — interlocks confirm contact pressure.
2. **Apply bath** — flood the examination surfaces.
3. **Fire the shots as the bath drains** — typically two shots of about half a second each, while a thinning film of suspension still lets particles migrate to leakage fields. Sequenced circular-then-longitudinal or multidirectional shots happen here.
4. **Transfer to viewing** — a darkened UV-A station, or an automated camera/detection station.
5. **Demagnetize** — pass-through AC coil or decaying-field station.
6. **Unload** — with accepted and rejected parts already separated.

Indexing machines run these stations **simultaneously on successive parts**: while one part is being shot, the previous one is under the lamps and the next is loading.

Timing faults are inspection faults. Fire too early and bath flow washes forming indications away; fire after the surface drains dry and particles can no longer migrate. That is why shot timers, bath valves, and conveyor indexing are procedure-controlled settings, not operator preferences.

sections.caption: An automated stationary unit sequences load, bath, shots, viewing, demag, and unload while a process monitor compares every shot and condition against limits and routes suspect parts to a reject lane.

sections.heading: Controls, Alarms, and Rejection Mechanisms
sections.body:
**Control and operation factors.** Current is set by transformer tap switches or stepless electronic (SCR) control and read on an ammeter — and the ammeter matters: HWDC readings depend on whether the meter responds to average or RMS values, so set currents on the basis your procedure specifies. Shot duration, shot count, and bath-valve timing run on timers. **Quick-break** circuitry collapses coil shots abruptly to preserve sensitivity near part ends on longitudinal magnetization. Interlocks block a shot until clamp pressure is made — protecting both the part (arc burns) and the data (low-current shots). Duty-cycle limits, contact-pad condition, and continuous bath agitation are the quiet variables an operator owns.

**Alarm and rejection mechanisms.** An automated system must refuse to pass parts it did not actually test:

- Each shot's **delivered current** is compared to preset limits; a low or missed shot trips an alarm, stops the index, or routes the part out.
- **Bath flow and agitation sensors** and **UV-A intensity monitors** alarm when application or viewing conditions drift out of specification.
- Rejected parts are **marked** (ink or paint) or **diverted** by gates into a reject/retest lane so they cannot mix with accepted product.

The governing rule: an alarm does not condemn just one part. Every part processed **since the last good verification** is suspect and gets re-tested. Fail-safe means a machine fault can never quietly become an escape.

sections.title: Arc flash lives at the contacts
sections.body: Head shots and prods push thousands of amperes through mechanical contacts. **Never make or break contact while current is flowing** — that is how arc flash, arc burns, and ignition sources happen. Clamp firmly on clean, well-maintained pads or braids, fire, and release only after the shot ends. Arc burns are rejectable damage in their own right, and the localized hard spot they leave can crack. The discipline scales from a 6,000 A bench all the way down to a prod kit: current off before contact moves.

figures.title: MT equipment classes: portable, mobile, stationary
Automated stationary unit: sequence, monitoring, rejection

checkQuestions.question: Portable MT equipment exists primarily because:
options: Portable units produce stronger fields than stationary benches
Many parts and structures cannot be brought to a stationary unit, so the test must travel to them
Yokes are more sensitive than wet benches for every flaw type
Portable units are exempt from performance checks
checkQuestions.explanation: Bridges, pipelines, vessels, and erected structures cannot be moved to a bench — the reason for portable gear is access. The trade-offs are lower amperage, lower duty cycle, and section-by-section coverage.

checkQuestions.question: A procedure calls for 100 A per inch of prod spacing. With prods set at 7 in., the smallest current source that can deliver the shot is:
options: An AC yoke with a 10 lb lifting check
A 500 A portable pack
A portable pack rated around 750–1,500 A
Only a 6,000 A stationary bench
checkQuestions.explanation: 7 in. × 100 A/in. = 700 A, which a typical portable prod pack in the 750–1,500 A class can supply. A yoke passes flux, not current through the part, and a 500 A unit falls short.

checkQuestions.question: Using a mobile power pack, you wrap the cable several turns around a long shaft and fire. The field produced and the discontinuities it finds best are:
options: Circular field — longitudinal cracks
Longitudinal field along the shaft — transverse cracks
Longitudinal field — seams parallel to the axis
No usable field — cables only work with bench coils
checkQuestions.explanation: A cable wrap is a coil: it drives flux lengthwise through the shaft (longitudinal magnetization), and leakage is strongest where flux crosses a flaw at 45–90° — transverse discontinuities. Axis-parallel seams need a circular field.

checkQuestions.question: In an automated continuous-method sequence, the magnetizing shots fire:
options: Before bath application, so the surface stays dry
As the bath drains off, while particles are still mobile — typically two shots of about half a second
Only after the surface has fully dried, to avoid washing indications away
During the demagnetization station
checkQuestions.explanation: Continuous method means field and mobile particles together: bath first, then shots while the draining film still lets particles migrate to leakage fields. Too early washes indications off; too late and particles cannot move.

checkQuestions.question: Mid-shift, the shot-current monitor on an automated bench alarms low — a contact pad had been burning for some unknown time. The correct disposition is:
options: Re-test only the single part that triggered the alarm
Raise the tap setting and keep running
Stop, correct the fault, and re-test every part processed since the last good system verification
Disable the monitor if the parts look clean
checkQuestions.explanation: A monitoring fault makes every part since the last proven-good check suspect — low-current shots may have passed untested parts. Fail-safe operation means quarantine and re-test, never silencing the alarm.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §4.1–4.2 (portable and mobile equipment)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §4.3 (stationary equipment, automation, sequential operations, alarms)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7 address equipment and system checks (generic reference)
Typical values: yoke lift ≈ 10 lb AC / 40 lb DC; prods ≈ 90–125 A per inch of spacing
